channel.title: Nicotine Science and Policy
channel.description: Daily digest of views, debates and discussions on nicotine science, policy, regulation and advocacy.

item.title: Government Announces Vape "Crackdown"
item.description: What happens when the easiest product to punish is also one of the tools helping people leave cigarettes behind? The government’s vape crackdown may target real concerns, but the consequences will depend on whether it separates illegal youth sales from adult harm reduction. Make vaping harder to access, understand or trust, and some smokers may simply stay with cigarettes. Tough policy is not the same as effective policy. (Published on July 16, 2026byplanetofthevapes.co.uk)

item.title: It’s Time for a More Honest Tobacco Policy
item.description: Honest tobacco policy begins with admitting that cigarettes are not dangerous because they contain nicotine, but because they burn. Once that distinction is blurred, everything else becomes distorted: risk communication, regulation and the choices offered to smokers. Adults deserve clear information about lower-risk alternatives, not a public health message that treats all nicotine use as the same. If policy is serious about reducing disease, it must be honest about where the harm comes from. (Published on July 17, 2026byrealclearhealth.com)

item.title: ASH Scotland Attacks Vapes
item.description: Attacking vaping may win headlines, but it can lose sight of the smoker. If public health messaging makes lower-risk products look as dangerous as cigarettes, the person most likely to be harmed is the adult who never switches. Vapes are not risk-free and should be regulated responsibly, especially around youth access. But turning them into a villain can leave the deadliest product looking like the familiar one. (Published on July 17, 2026byplanetofthevapes.co.uk)

item.title: Fact Sheet: Arguments against "Youth Epidemic" Claims
item.description: Youth protection is essential, but it should not be used as a blanket argument against harm reduction for adults. Policies aimed at preventing underage use need to be judged by evidence, not assumptions about what sounds protective. If restrictions make safer nicotine products harder for adult smokers to access, they may create new harms while doing little to address the real drivers of youth use. Good policy can protect young people without closing the door on adults trying to leave cigarettes behind. (Published on July 17, 2026bysubstack.com)
